content:
# John Chapter 15 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 15 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 15:1 — "vine" vs. "grapevine" and "vinedresser" vs. "gardener"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am the true vine, and the Father of me is the vinedresser" | Literal ampelos and geOrgos |
| LOGOS | "I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser" | Same terms, natural English possessives |
| PNEUMA | "I am the true grapevine, and my Father is the gardener" | "Grapevine" specifies; "gardener" generalizes geOrgos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "vine" and "vinedresser." "Vine" is sufficient — "grapevine" is redundant given the context. "Vinedresser" (geOrgos in the context of a vineyard) is more precise than PNEUMA's generic "gardener."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:2 — "takes away" vs. "cuts off" for airO

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not bearing fruit, he takes away" | Literal airei — "takes away/lifts up" |
| LOGOS | "does not bear fruit he takes away" | Same rendering |
| PNEUMA | "He cuts off every branch of mine that doesn't produce fruit" | "Cuts off" — more violent than airei; adds "of mine" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "takes away." The Greek airei can mean "take away," "lift up," or "remove" — it is deliberately ambiguous. "Cuts off" (PNEUMA) imports a specificity not in the word and eliminates the possible reading of "lifts up" (i.e., tends to).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. The ambiguity of airei should be preserved.

---

## 15:3 — "clean because of the word" vs. "pruned and purified by the message"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Already you are clean because of the word that I have spoken to you" | Literal katharoi; dia ton logon |
| LOGOS | "Already you are clean because of the word that I have spoken to you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "You have already been pruned and purified by the message I have given you" | "Pruned and purified" interprets katharoi with vineyard imagery; "message" for logos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Clean" preserves the wordplay with "prunes" (kathairei) in v.2 — both from the root katharos. PNEUMA makes the connection explicit but loses the subtlety of the pun.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "message" also replaces the theologically loaded logos.

---

## 15:4 — "Abide" vs. "Remain"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Abide in me, and I in you" | "Abide" for menO |
| LOGOS | "Abide in me, and I in you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Remain in me, and I will remain in you" | "Remain" for menO; adds "will" making Jesus' abiding conditional on theirs |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Abide" (EMET/LOGOS). "Abide" is the traditional Johannine term and carries theological depth. PNEUMA's "Remain" is acceptable but "abide" is preferred for its association with Johannine theology. Critically, PNEUMA's addition of "will" ("I will remain in you") introduces conditionality — the Greek is simply "and I in you" (kagO en hymin), a parallel imperative, not a conditional promise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The "will" in PNEUMA's rendering introduces a theological problem.

---

## 15:5 — "for apart from me you can do nothing" vs. "you are not able to do nothing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "apart from me you are not able to do nothing" | Double negative preserving Greek ou... ouden |
| LOGOS | "apart from me you can do nothing" | Single negative in English; same meaning |
| PNEUMA | "apart from me you can do nothing" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA's single negative. English double negatives create the opposite meaning ("can't do nothing" = "can do something"). EMET's attempt to preserve the Greek intensifying double negative fails in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 15:6 — "thrown away like a branch" vs. "cast out as the branch" vs. "thrown away like a useless branch"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he is cast out as the branch and is withered" | Literal eblethE exO; passive; "the branch" with article |
| LOGOS | "he is thrown away like a branch and withers" | "Like" — comparative; active "withers" |
| PNEUMA | "thrown away like a useless branch and withers. Such branches are gathered into a pile to be burned." | Adds "useless"; "pile" not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Thrown away like a branch" is clear. PNEUMA adds "useless" (not in the Greek) and "pile" (the Greek simply says "they gather them").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:8 — "so prove to be my disciples" vs. "become disciples of me" vs. "you are my true disciples"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that you bear much fruit and become disciples of me" | Literal genEsthe = "become" |
| LOGOS | "that you bear much fruit and so prove to be my disciples" | "Prove to be" — fruit-bearing demonstrates discipleship |
| PNEUMA | "When you produce much fruit, you are my true disciples. This brings great glory to my Father." | Restructures entirely; "true disciples" not in Greek; splits into two sentences |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "so prove to be my disciples." The Greek genEsthe can mean "become" or "show yourselves to be." In context, fruit-bearing demonstrates existing discipleship rather than creating it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's restructuring and addition of "true" go beyond the text.

---

## 15:10 — "keep" vs. "obey" for tereO

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If you keep the commandments of me" | Literal tereO = "keep/guard" |
| LOGOS | "If you keep my commandments" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "When you obey my commandments" | "Obey" for tereO; "When" changes conditional to temporal |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "keep." TereO means to guard, keep, preserve — broader than mere obedience. It implies treasuring and holding fast. Additionally, "If" (ean) must remain conditional, not temporal ("when").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "when" removes the conditional element and "obey" narrows tereO.

---

## 15:11 — "that your joy may be full" vs. "your joy will overflow!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the joy of you may be made full" | Literal plErOthE (subjunctive passive) |
| LOGOS | "that your joy may be full" | Natural English subjunctive |
| PNEUMA | "so that you will be filled with my joy. Yes, your joy will overflow!" | "Overflow" goes beyond plEroO (fill/complete); adds exclamation and "Yes" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PlEroO means "fill/make full/complete" — not "overflow." PNEUMA's "overflow" is a different word (hyperperisseuO). The subjunctive "may be" preserves the purposive hina clause.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "overflow" exceeds the semantic range of plEroO.

---

## 15:15 — "servants" vs. "slaves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "No longer do I call you slaves, because the slave does not know what the lord of him does" | doulos = "slave" (literal) |
| LOGOS | "No longer do I call you servants, for the servant does not know what his master is doing" | doulos = "servant" (traditional) |
| PNEUMA | "I no longer call you slaves, because a master doesn't confide in his slaves" | "Slaves" with EMET; "confide" interprets "does not know what his master is doing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "servants." While "slaves" is technically more accurate for doulos, the context is about relational status (servant vs. friend), and "servant" is the traditional rendering in this specific passage. PNEUMA's "confide" is an interesting interpretation but adds a word not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The servant/slave translation question is noted as recurring throughout John.

---

## 15:20 — "A servant is not greater than his master" vs. "A slave is not greater than the lord of him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A slave is not greater than the lord of him" | doulos = slave; kurios = lord (literal) |
| LOGOS | "A servant is not greater than his master" | Traditional rendering echoing 13:16 |
| PNEUMA | "A slave is not greater than the master" | "Slave" with EMET; "master" simplifies |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering for consistency with 13:16.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:21 — "on account of my name" vs. "because of me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because of the name of me" | Literal dia to onoma mou |
| LOGOS | "on account of my name" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "because of me" | Simplifies — drops "name" entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "on account of my name." The "name" (onoma) is theologically significant — persecution comes because of who Jesus is (his name/identity), not merely because of association with him.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's omission of "name" loses an important concept.

---

## 15:22 — "they would not have been guilty of sin" vs. "sin they would not have"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sin they would not have" | Literal hamartian ouk eichon; inverted word order |
| LOGOS | "they would not have been guilty of sin" | Adds "guilty of" — interpretive expansion |
| PNEUMA | "They would not be guilty" | Drops "sin" noun; "guilty" alone |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Guilty of sin" is a justified English expansion of the terse Greek "would not have sin" — in English, "having sin" requires clarification.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:24 — "the works that no one else did" vs. "such miraculous signs"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the works I had not done among them that no one else did" | Literal ta erga |
| LOGOS | "the works that no one else did" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "such miraculous signs among them that no one else could do" | "Miraculous signs" interprets erga; adds "could" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "works." John uses erga (works), not sEmeia (signs) here — the distinction matters in Johannine theology.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's substitution of "miraculous signs" for "works" conflates two distinct Johannine terms.

---

## 15:25 — "their Law" vs. "their Scriptures"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the law of them" | Literal en tO nomO autOn |
| LOGOS | "their Law" | Capitalizes Law; preserves nomO |
| PNEUMA | "their Scriptures" | "Scriptures" replaces "Law" — broadens the reference |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "their Law." Jesus uses nomos here (citing Psalm 35:19 or 69:4), using "Law" broadly to mean Scripture — but the word choice is deliberate and should be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:26 — "Advocate" vs. "Helper" and "bear witness" vs. "testify"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "When the Advocate comes... that [one] will testify concerning me" | "Advocate"; "testify" for martureO |
| LOGOS | "when the Helper comes... he will bear witness about me" | "Helper"; "bear witness" |
| PNEUMA | "I will send you the Advocate — the Spirit of truth. He will come to you from the Father and will testify all about me." | "Advocate"; restructures — makes Jesus the sender more prominent; "all about" adds scope |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Advocate" (per the 14:16 decision) with "bear witness about me" (LOGOS phrasing). "Bear witness" preserves the legal/forensic tone that matches "Advocate."

**Status:** Resolved primarily in favor of LOGOS's sentence structure with "Advocate" substituted. PNEUMA's "all about me" adds scope not in the Greek.

---

## 15:27 — "from the beginning" vs. "from the beginning of my ministry"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "because from [the] beginning you are with me" | Literal ap' archEs; present tense "are" |
| LOGOS | "because you have been with me from the beginning" | Present perfect "have been" |
| PNEUMA | "because you have been with me from the beginning of my ministry" | Adds "of my ministry" — interprets archE |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "From the beginning" is sufficient — archE here refers to the beginning of Jesus' ministry, but specifying this narrows what John leaves compact.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition is interpretively correct but textually unwarranted.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding interpretive content | 6 | "useless" (v.6), "true" (v.8), "miraculous" (v.24), "of my ministry" (v.27), "pile" (v.6), "all about" (v.26) |
| Key Johannine term substitution | 4 | menO: abide/remain (v.4), tereO: keep/obey (v.10), erga: works/signs (v.24), nomos: Law/Scriptures (v.25) |
| PNEUMA changing grammatical mood | 2 | Conditional to temporal (v.10 "when" for "if"), subjunctive to indicative (v.11 "will overflow") |
| Servant/slave (doulos) translation | 2 | vv.15, 20 — recurring across John |
| EMET awkward English syntax | 3 | Double negative (v.5), inverted word order (vv.18, 22) |
| LOGOS departing from SOPHIA consensus | 1 | "Helper" vs. "Advocate" (v.26) — consistent with ch.14 decision |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 15 shows the vine allegory producing fewer divergences in the first half (vv.1-11) because the language is concrete and imagistic — all three translators track the metaphor closely. The divergences increase in the second half (vv.18-27) where abstract theological concepts (sin, guilt, testimony) offer more room for interpretive variation.

The most significant pattern in this chapter is PNEUMA's tendency to change grammatical moods: converting conditionals ("if") to temporals ("when") in v.10, and subjunctive purpose ("may be full") to indicative certainty ("will overflow") in v.11. These shifts subtly alter the theology — making contingent promises into guarantees.

The menO/abide question (v.4) is a chapter-defining translation choice. SOPHIA sides with EMET/LOGOS in using "abide" rather than PNEUMA's "remain," recognizing that "abide" carries the full weight of Johannine theological tradition. Additionally, PNEUMA's insertion of "will" into Jesus' reciprocal abiding ("I will remain in you") introduces conditionality into what the Greek presents as a parallel imperative.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
